text: The surgeon completes the dissection in the right retro-rectus space, separating the rectus muscle from the posterior sheath.
The surgeon has inserted ports in the left lateral retro-rectus space, positioned medial to the left linea semilunaris.
The surgeon has completed the incision of the right posterior rectus sheath and is now developing the right retro-rectus space, preparing for the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.
The surgeon approaches the area below the arcuate line, where the posterior rectus sheath visibly begins to attenuate.
The surgeon demonstrates the attenuation of the posterior rectus sheath, highlighting the visible thinning of the sheath layers.
Below the arcuate line, the surgeon reveals multiple layers beyond the peritoneum, demonstrating the complex anatomy in this region.
The surgeon initiates a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) by approaching the arcuate line, focusing on the area where the posterior rectus sheath has been previously incised.
The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space, where the preperitoneal fat is seen separating easily from the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs dissection in the inguinal region, although minimal dissection is required at this stage.
The surgeon identifies a second arcuate line in the attenuated posterior rectus tissue, highlighting the distinct anatomical feature within the surgical field.
The surgeon enters the desired space, revealing the preperitoneal fat that separates laterally from the abdominal wall. The transverse abdominis muscle is clearly visible.
The surgeon carefully sweeps the peritoneum off the posterior rectus sheath to facilitate the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.

The surgeon enters the space where the preperitoneal fat separates from the transverse abdominis, sweeping the fat and peritoneum off the muscle. The linea semilunaris is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the linea semilunaris and sweeps the fat off the transverse abdominis, demonstrating how easily the fat separates from the underlying tissue.

The surgeon identifies small vessels within the surgical field and uses cautery to manage them. The surgeon demonstrates a technique of sweeping tissue and inspecting the plane, highlighting the clear and well-defined dissection plane.
The surgeon encounters the linea semilinearis and proceeds to complete the dissection in this area, focusing on separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon approaches the seam of the linea alba during stage one of the ETEP procedure, ensuring precise alignment where the anterior and posterior rectus sheaths converge.
The surgeon identifies the seam where the posterior lamella, anterior lamella, and anterior oblique converge or diverge within the linea alba.

The surgeon identifies neurovascular bundles as landmarks near the linea semilinare, indicating proximity to this anatomical seam. The arcuate line is visible, guiding the surgeon's awareness of the seam location.
The surgeon ensures hemostasis by addressing any bleeding areas within the surgical field, focusing on the transversus abdominis release (TAR) region.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the posterior rectus sheath, entering the plane between the preperitoneal fat and the posterior rectus sheath. The fat is removed to expose the peritoneum, allowing for rapid division of the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon points out the seam of the linea semilunaris, maintaining a safe distance of about one centimeter from it. The posterior rectus sheath is pulled and divided, with caution to prevent medialization of the linea semilunaris.

The surgeon observes that the linea semilunaris is not medializing significantly in this patient, allowing for a straightforward division of the posterior rectus sheath. The posterior rectus sheath is composed of the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis aponeurosis.
The surgeon has fully dissected the retrorectus space, clearly identifying the linea semilunaris to avoid injury during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the posterior rectus sheath in the lower half of the surgical field, using a TAR (transversus abdominis release) approach. The tissue is separated cleanly without complications.
The surgeon carefully ensures the peritoneum is separated from the posterior rectus sheath before dividing it, avoiding accidental perforation of the peritoneum during the ETEP Reef Stoppa procedure.
The surgeon moves laterally, entering a different anatomical plane closer to the peritoneum, which is typically accessed during inguinal hernia repairs.

The surgeon leaves the fat layer adjacent to the transverse abdominis muscle, carefully approaching the peritoneum. This technique is applied during a full transversus abdominis release (TAR) as the dissection transitions into the lower abdomen and pelvis.
The surgeon positions the dissection plane directly on the testicular vessels and the vas deferens, avoiding a lateral or posterior approach to these structures.

The surgeon observes that the peritoneum is not separating well from the transversalis fascia. The procedure is transitioning into the space between the transversalis fascia and the transverse abdominis.

On the right side, the transverse salus fascia is visibly lifting off the transverse abdominus muscle. The surgeon transitions into the pre-transverse salus fascia plane, moving away from the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon navigates through the upper abdomen, maintaining proximity to the transverse abdominus muscle, progressing towards the costal margin.

The surgeon maintains dissection against the diaphragm muscle, following the same plane while maneuvering under the ribs, performing a bottoms-up TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release) technique to elevate the dissection as high as possible.
The surgeon navigates between the transverse salus fascia and the transversus abdominis muscle, switching to a top-down TAR approach as the dissection becomes more challenging in this area.
The surgeon continues dissection in the pre-transverse salus fascia plane within the upper abdomen, maintaining this plane despite the appearance of the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon carefully moves the bowel aside to provide a clearer view and safer access for the transversus abdominis release (TAR), revealing the posterior rectal sheath resting on the bowel.
The surgeon performs a top-down TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release), cutting the posterior lumbar portion of the internal oblique muscle followed by the muscular transversus abdominis.

The surgeon carefully navigates the medial aspect of the surgical field, ensuring not to disrupt the diaphragm contribution while maintaining the dissection plane between the transversus abdominis and diaphragm. The view is directed downward to confirm the absence of muscle on the underlying surface.

The surgeon navigates around the xiphoid process, carefully dissecting behind the xiphoid fat. Attention is given to the edges of the xiphoid where vessels are prone to bleeding, while identifying the diaphragm's location.
The surgeon checks the position of the diaphragm to ensure it is not below the surgical field before proceeding to open the area, revealing the diaphragm's presence.
The surgeon confirms the correct surgical plane adjacent to the diaphragm, revealing the muscle fibers of the diaphragm. The area appears aerial with a cotton candy-like texture.
The surgeon scores the path along the thick transverse abdominus muscle, preparing for dissection while ensuring precision to avoid deviation.
The surgeon uses one edge of the scissor to shimmy into the transverse abdominus muscle, lifting it slightly before applying cautery to carefully cut through the muscle fibers.
The surgeon connects the top-down transverse abdominus release (TAR) to the bottom-up TAR, with visible bleeding from the muscle requiring spot coagulation.

The surgeon is positioned under the diaphragm on the right side, while on the left side, the space is not yet fully developed. The goal is to get close to the muscle, facilitating the TAR (transversus abdominis release) procedure laterally.
The surgeon aims to position against both the diaphragm and transverse abdominis muscles, dividing the bridging fascia between them, which appears fatty in the lateral region.
The surgeon maintains dissection right up against the transverse abdominus muscle, navigating through the pre-transverse salus plane despite the tissue planes suggesting a pre-particular approach.
The surgeon completes the division of the transversus abdominis muscle using cautery, then proceeds with retromuscular dissection, maintaining close proximity to the muscle tissue.

The surgeon encounters interdigitating fibers from the transversus abdominis fascia between the muscle, carefully pulling the colon away to facilitate the TAR procedure. This maneuver aims to avoid excessive cautery near the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum.
The surgeon carefully hugs the muscle while cauterizing along the edge of the muscle, avoiding the transversalis fascia side, to maintain precision during the procedure.
The surgeon navigates the space between the muscle and the transversalis fascia, using cautery at the edge of the muscle to maintain hemostasis and expedite the procedure.
The surgeon cauterizes along the edge of the muscle, demonstrating the varying extent of dissection in this area.
In a bilateral TAR procedure, the surgeon dissects until the peritoneum and posterior rectus sheath lay on the bowel. The dissection progresses laterally, entering a different anatomical space.

The surgeon identifies two distinct fat layers and chooses to stay in the surgical plane closer to the peritoneum. As the dissection progresses laterally, the focus remains on maintaining proximity to the peritoneum rather than the muscle.

The surgeon assesses the advancement in the posterior layer, noting tension and proceeding to divide the tissue. With good retraction using the left hand, the tissue appears like cotton candy, facilitating division while remaining on the peritoneum side.

The surgeon provides an overview of the surgical field after completing the transversus abdominis release (TAR), showing the posterior rectus sheath reaching the midline. In the lower part of the field, additional dissection is performed, resembling a transabdominal preperitoneal (TAP) approach, with a different visual perspective.
The surgeon uses left hand retraction to peel the peritoneum off the posterior structures, creating a visible 'cotton candy' tissue effect that guides the dissection plane.
The surgeon lifts the tissue towards the umbilical region, creating a cotton candy-like appearance. The obturator space is visible, with a small valley indicating the plane for dissection.
The surgeon uses their left hand to provide exposure, enhancing visualization of the dissection path, allowing precise tissue separation.

The surgeon observes that the patient's round ligament has been previously divided, allowing the posterior rectus sheath to be medialized significantly, facilitating the closure of the posterior layer.

The surgeon prepares to place three new ports and redock the surgical instruments to initiate the transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the left side. The left retroactive space has already been dissected and opened.

The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line, cutting the attenuated posterior rectus sheath in the left pelvis area. The surgeon's hand grasps the peritoneum while identifying the plane where preperitoneal fat separates from the transversus abdominis. The linea semilunaris is shown.
The surgeon displays the linea semilunaris while performing an inguinal dissection, carefully hugging the peritoneum to maintain proximity to the anatomical structures.
The surgeon elevates tissue near the pubis with the left hand, revealing remnants of the round ligament, which appears partially divided.

The surgeon uses the left hand to retract and expose the tissue plane, allowing clear visualization of the area. The scissors are used as a hot blade to carefully dissect along the visible tissue planes without extensive exploration.
The surgeon scores the tissue to open up the planes, revealing a plane where the preperitoneal fat separates from the transverse abdominis. A large cavity is created in the area.

The surgeon sweeps the preperitoneal fat and peritoneum off the posterior rectus sheath, revealing the sheath clearly. The linea semilunaris is visible, ensuring it remains uninjured during the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the ease of separating the preperitoneal fat from the posterior rectus sheath, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses cautery to divide small vessels in the lateral space to prevent bleeding, while sweeping the peritoneum off the underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the linea semilunaris and uses cautery to divide small vessels, preventing bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses cautery to divide small vessels near the linea semilunaris. A new port is placed medial to the linea semilunaris, originating from the ETEP approach.

The surgeon performs preperitoneal dissection and divides the posterior rectus sheath, alternating between these techniques. The preperitoneal dissection varies in difficulty, sometimes allowing for rapid progress and other times requiring slower, careful movements.

The surgeon adjusts the lighting to reduce brightness on the aerial tissue. The peritoneum appears adhered to the transverse salus fascia, making separation challenging. The surgeon transitions into the pre-transverse salus fascia plane to facilitate dissection.

The surgeon carefully pushes the transversalis fascia off, revealing the neurovascular bundle and staying medial to it. As the dissection progresses, the posterior rectus sheath becomes visible, and the surgeon divides it before fully detaching the peritoneum.

The surgeon assesses the surgical field to determine the appropriate dissection plane, aiming to be close to the muscle tissue. The presence of blood complicates the visibility and ease of identifying the correct plane.

The surgeon is in the correct plane on the right side and is attempting to join it with the left side. Using cautery, the surgeon applies good left-hand retraction, causing the tissue to visibly separate and pop.

The surgeon uses left hand retraction to expose tissue, allowing it to separate visibly with the application of cautery. The dissection progresses from the bottom up, with occasional checks to ensure alignment with the correct anatomical path.
The surgeon begins dissection into the muscular portion of the transverse abdominis, carefully maintaining proximity to the muscle fibers.

The surgeon decides to proceed with a top-down approach, dividing the posterior lamella of the internal oblique muscle. The path is scored to ensure precise navigation and prevent deviation from the intended surgical plane.
The surgeon uses cautery aggressively on the thick transverse abdominis muscle, inserting scissors behind the muscle to facilitate tissue advancement. The dissection progresses from right to left.

The surgeon reaches the muscular portion of the transverse abdominis, inserts scissors to lift the tissue, and uses cautery. After scoring through the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, the surgeon continues in a bottom-up fashion to complete the TAR (transversus abdominis release) on the patient's left side.

The surgeon carefully explores the surgical field to ensure the dissection plane is between the diaphragm and the transversalis fascia, avoiding accidental entry into incorrect anatomical layers. The diaphragm is observed to be fused to the posterior rectus sheath, facilitating the dissection process.

The surgeon identifies the areolar plane adjacent to the diaphragm muscle, facilitating the continuation of the transversus abdominis release (TAR). The dissection proceeds by maintaining proximity to the transverse abdominis muscle and the diaphragm, joining the two anatomical spaces.
The surgeon uses cautery along the edge of the muscle to complete the division of the transverse abdominis. Following this, the surgeon proceeds with the retromuscular dissection.

The surgeon navigates through the areolar tissue, encountering less transverse salus fascia between the muscle layers. The procedure is completed when the peritoneum and posterior rectus sheath rest on the bowel, and the edges of the posterior rectus sheath meet.
